title: What is MySQL's EXPLAIN ANALYZE?
description: How does EXPLAIN ANALYZE differ from EXPLAIN?
options: EXPLAIN ANALYZE (MySQL 8.0.18+) actually executes the query and shows real timing alongside estimates; provides actual rows, loops, and execution time per operation. EXPLAIN only shows estimates without running the query
correct_answer: EXPLAIN ANALYZE (MySQL 8.0.18+) actually executes the query and shows real timing alongside estimates; provides actual rows, loops, and execution time per operation. EXPLAIN only shows estimates without running the query

title: What is the difference between BLOB and TEXT in MySQL?
description: Compare BLOB and TEXT column types.
options: Both store large data; BLOB stores binary data (images, files) with byte-based comparison/sorting; TEXT stores character data with charset-based collation. Variants: TINY, MEDIUM, LONG (up to 4GB). Cannot have default values
correct_answer: Both store large data; BLOB stores binary data (images, files) with byte-based comparison/sorting; TEXT stores character data with charset-based collation. Variants: TINY, MEDIUM, LONG (up to 4GB). Cannot have default values

title: Write a query to implement row-level security.
correct_answer:
-- Create a view that filters rows based on current user
CREATE VIEW my_documents AS
SELECT d.*
FROM documents d
JOIN user_permissions up ON d.department_id = up.department_id
WHERE up.username = CURRENT_USER()
  AND up.can_read = TRUE;

-- Users query the view instead of the table
-- GRANT SELECT ON my_documents TO 'app_user'@'%';

title: What is MySQL's invisible index feature?
options: ALTER TABLE t ALTER INDEX idx INVISIBLE makes the optimizer ignore the index without dropping it; useful for testing if an index is still needed. ALTER INDEX idx VISIBLE re-enables it. Introduced in MySQL 8.0
correct_answer: ALTER TABLE t ALTER INDEX idx INVISIBLE makes the optimizer ignore the index without dropping it; useful for testing if an index is still needed. ALTER INDEX idx VISIBLE re-enables it. Introduced in MySQL 8.0

title: Write a query using JSON_TABLE to parse JSON arrays.
description: Extract structured data from JSON columns.
correct_answer:
SELECT p.id, p.name, jt.skill, jt.level
FROM profiles p,
JSON_TABLE(
  p.skills,
  '$[*]' COLUMNS (
    skill VARCHAR(100) PATH '$.name',
    level INT PATH '$.level'
  )
) AS jt
WHERE jt.level >= 3
ORDER BY jt.level DESC;

title: What is MySQL's innodb_buffer_pool_size?
description: How does buffer pool size affect performance?
options: InnoDB buffer pool caches table data and indexes in memory; recommend 70-80% of available RAM for dedicated MySQL servers. Larger pool = fewer disk reads = better performance. Monitor with SHOW STATUS LIKE 'Innodb_buffer_pool%'
correct_answer: InnoDB buffer pool caches table data and indexes in memory; recommend 70-80% of available RAM for dedicated MySQL servers. Larger pool = fewer disk reads = better performance. Monitor with SHOW STATUS LIKE 'Innodb_buffer_pool%'

title: Write a query to generate a calendar table.
correct_answer:
WITH RECURSIVE dates AS (
  SELECT DATE('2024-01-01') AS dt
  UNION ALL
  SELECT dt + INTERVAL 1 DAY FROM dates WHERE dt < '2024-12-31'
)
SELECT
  dt AS date,
  YEAR(dt) AS year,
  MONTH(dt) AS month,
  DAY(dt) AS day,
  DAYNAME(dt) AS day_name,
  WEEKDAY(dt) AS weekday,
  WEEK(dt) AS week_number,
  QUARTER(dt) AS quarter
FROM dates;

title: What is MySQL's multi-version concurrency control (MVCC)?
description: How does MVCC work in InnoDB?
options: MVCC allows readers to see a consistent snapshot without locking writers; InnoDB keeps multiple versions of rows using undo logs. READ COMMITTED sees latest committed data; REPEATABLE READ sees snapshot from transaction start
correct_answer: MVCC allows readers to see a consistent snapshot without locking writers; InnoDB keeps multiple versions of rows using undo logs. READ COMMITTED sees latest committed data; REPEATABLE READ sees snapshot from transaction start

title: Write a query to detect hot spots in time-series data.
description: Find periods with abnormally high activity.
correct_answer:
WITH hourly AS (
  SELECT DATE_FORMAT(created_at, '%Y-%m-%d %H:00:00') AS hour,
         COUNT(*) AS event_count
  FROM events
  GROUP BY hour
),
stats AS (
  SELECT AVG(event_count) AS avg_count,
         STDDEV(event_count) AS std_count
  FROM hourly
)
SELECT h.hour, h.event_count,
       ROUND((h.event_count - s.avg_count) / s.std_count, 2) AS z_score
FROM hourly h, stats s
WHERE h.event_count > s.avg_count + 2 * s.std_count
ORDER BY h.event_count DESC;

title: What is the difference between LIKE and REGEXP in MySQL?
description: Compare LIKE and REGEXP for pattern matching.
options: LIKE uses simple wildcards (% for any chars, _ for single char), is faster, and is index-friendly with left-anchored patterns. REGEXP/RLIKE uses full regular expressions but can't use indexes. Use LIKE for simple prefix matches, REGEXP for complex patterns
correct_answer: LIKE uses simple wildcards (% for any chars, _ for single char), is faster, and is index-friendly with left-anchored patterns. REGEXP/RLIKE uses full regular expressions but can't use indexes. Use LIKE for simple prefix matches, REGEXP for complex patterns

title: Write a query to build a hierarchical tree using recursive CTE.
correct_answer:
WITH RECURSIVE org_tree AS (
  -- Base case: root nodes (no parent)
  SELECT id, name, parent_id, 0 AS depth,
         CAST(name AS CHAR(1000)) AS path
  FROM departments
  WHERE parent_id IS NULL
  
  UNION ALL
  
  -- Recursive case
  SELECT d.id, d.name, d.parent_id, t.depth + 1,
         CONCAT(t.path, ' > ', d.name)
  FROM departments d
  JOIN org_tree t ON d.parent_id = t.id
)
SELECT * FROM org_tree ORDER BY path;

title: What is MySQL's character set and collation system?
description: How do charset and collation affect queries?
options: Character set defines which characters can be stored (utf8mb4 for full Unicode); collation defines sort/comparison rules (_ci = case-insensitive, _cs = case-sensitive, _bin = binary). Set at server, database, table, or column level
correct_answer: Character set defines which characters can be stored (utf8mb4 for full Unicode); collation defines sort/comparison rules (_ci = case-insensitive, _cs = case-sensitive, _bin = binary). Set at server, database, table, or column level

title: Write a query to implement soft delete with automatic cleanup.
description: Implement soft delete with a scheduled purge.
